explanation:
The requirement in natural language says that the route-calculation message (represented by the atomic proposition 'routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi') must be sent only after querying four specific components: Neighbor Set, 2 Hop Neighbor Set, Topology Information Base, and Multiple Interface Association Set. In terms of LTL, this means that 'routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' can only occur after all four queries have occurred. To capture this causality, we use the 'release' (R) operator: the formula states that the sending of the route-calculation message is 'released by' the conjunction of the four queries, i.e., as long as the message has not been sent, it is required that at least one of the required queries has not happened yet; once all queries have happened (in any order), only then can the 'routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' event be allowed. The formula is enforced globally (G) for all time steps, as required for every DM_i. 

Compared to simpler LTL properties that might only require each query to eventually happen before the route-calculation message (e.g., by specifying four 'until' properties), using releases in a single formula more accurately captures the exclusivity and strict ordering: the message cannot be sent until all four queries are made, and it does not allow the message to 'slip through' before any are done. This single formula avoids the risk of expressing weaker properties that could be satisfied if only some queries precede the message.

Thus, the most precise translation is: globally, 'routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' is only permitted after the queries 'local_node_queried_neighbor_set', 'local_node_queried_two_hop_neighbor_set', 'local_node_queried_topology_information_base', and 'local_node_queried_multiple_interface_association' have all occurred.

The input specifies that the 'route-calculation message' (represented here as 'routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi') must be sent only after the Local Node has queried the Neighbor Set, 2 Hop Neighbor Set, Topology Information Base, and Multiple Interface Association Set components. That is, at any moment when 'routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' is true, it must be preceded (possibly immediately) by all four queried events. This is best represented by stating that globally, whenever 'routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' occurs, then in the past, all the required 'local_node_queried_X' events occurred since the last route calculation message (or since the beginning if it's the first). LTL does not have explicit past operators, but the property can be enforced using implication and the 'Globally' operator: globally, if 'routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' occurs, then previously all: 'local_node_queried_neighbor_set', 'local_node_queried_two_hop_neighbor_set', 'local_node_queried_topology_information_base', and 'local_node_queried_multiple_interface_association' occurred since the last occurrence of 'routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi'. Alternatively, we can enforce that it is never the case that routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi happens before all four queries. This is commonly formalized using 'until' operators: globally, route calculation may not occur until all the queries have occurred. In LTL, since we can't say 'since' or use explicit past, we use for each instance that the routecalc is sent, it must have been preceded (possibly immediately) by all queries. The following property captures this tendency: whenever a routecalc is sent, before it, all the queries took place and none were missed. The property can be written as a safety property using implication and the release operator (R).
output_LTL: G(routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (F[0:1] local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F[0:1] local_node_queried_two_hop_neighbor_set & F[0:1] local_node_queried_topology_information_base & F[0:1] local_node_queried_multiple_interface_association))

The requirement for 'only after' is best represented as: globally, if routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, then in the past, all four queries have happened. In standard LTL without past operators, this is typically approximated by asserting that before sending the route calculation message, all those queries must have happened at some point before or at that step. Alternatively, we specify that until all four queries have happened, the routecalculation message cannot be sent. Thus, globally, until all four queries have occurred, routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi may not occur. After they have occurred (at least once), route calculations may be sent.

The input property requires that a route-calculation message must be sent only after the Local Node has queried four components: the Neighbor Set, 2 Hop Neighbor Set, Topology Information Base, and Multiple Interface Association Set. This ordering must hold globally and for every DM_i (assumedly, the atomic propositions are indexed or implicitly quantified, but in LTL we write general properties). To encode 'only after', for any point where a routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, it is required that there has previously occurred (somewhere in the past) a successful query of all four components, and between these four queries and the routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi, no new routecalc_msg can happen. In LTL (future-oriented), one common encoding is: 'it is always the case that if a routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, then all four queries must have occurred in the past, and since the last occurrence of any of the four queries, no routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurred'. However, LTL natively lacks past operators, so in future LTL this is usually encoded to enforce that the routecalc message can happen only after all four queries have happened. This can be represented by requiring the queries to happen first, and after that, eventually (but not before), the routecalc_msg occurs. For always-only-after (i.e., whenever one wants to send a routecalc message, it must be preceded by all four queries), the property is: globally, routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi implies that all four queries have happened in the past without an intervening routecalc_msg. This is commonly adhered to in practice by ensuring that until all queries, routecalc_msg cannot happen: G ( ( !local_node_queried_neighbor_set | !local_node_queried_two_hop_neighbor_set | !local_node_queried_topology_information_base | !local_node_queried_multiple_interface_association ) U routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi ) is not quite enough, but the following enforces that at any point, sending routecalc_msg implies all four have previously happened and not reset. In LTL without past, a common translation is: globally, whenever routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi, then previously all four queried propositions must have held true. Since we cannot use past operators, the simplest future LTL that captures 'must only be sent after' is: It is always the case that, if any of the four queries have not yet occurred, then a routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi cannot occur. That is: G ( ( !local_node_queried_neighbor_set | !local_node_queried_two_hop_neighbor_set | !local_node_queried_topology_information_base | !local_node_queried_multiple_interface_association ) -> !routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi ). 

This precisely captures the requirement that routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi cannot be sent unless queries to all four components have occurred (the four queried atomic propositions have become true). LTL without past does not allow us to state 'the last' or 'immediately after', but this is the standard way 'only after' is encoded in future temporal logic. The suggested formula is:

G ( !(routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U ( local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_two_hop_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_topology_information_base & local_node_queried_multiple_interface_association & routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi ) )

G( routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_two_hop_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_topology_information_base & local_node_queried_multiple_interface_association) )

The key requirement in the natural language is that the route-calculation message (represented by routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) must be sent only after (that is, not before) querying four components: Neighbor Set (local_node_queried_neighbor_set), 2 Hop Neighbor Set (local_node_queried_two_hop_neighbor_set), Topology Information Base (local_node_queried_topology_information_base), and Multiple Interface Association Set (local_node_queried_multiple_interface_association). To capture "must be sent only after querying all components," we require that whenever the routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, it is preceded, at some point, by queries to all four required sets. This is expressed in LTL by stating that globally, if routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, then in the past (as standard LTL does not have past operators, we use the equivalent: until operators), all four queries must have occurred before the routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi. More concretely, we can require that none of the routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi happens before all four have happened, by specifying that until all have occurred, routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi cannot happen.

Thus, the LTL formula is:
G ( (!routecalc_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_two_hop_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_topology_information_base & local_node_queried_multiple_interface_association) )

This says: globally, route-calc message may not be sent (i.e., must not occur) UNTIL all four queries have simultaneously occurred at least once.
